examples.title: ETL Process Pseudocode
examples.description: Demonstrates the basic ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) process flow in data warehousing
examples.code:
PROCEDURE ETL_Process
BEGIN
  // EXTRACT PHASE
  FOR EACH source_system IN source_systems
    raw_data = EXTRACT_FROM(source_system)
    STORE_IN_STAGING(raw_data)
  END FOR

  // TRANSFORM PHASE
  staging_data = LOAD_FROM_STAGING()
  
  FOR EACH record IN staging_data
    cleaned_record = CLEAN_DATA(record)
    validated_record = VALIDATE(cleaned_record)
    IF validated_record IS VALID THEN
      transformed_record = APPLY_TRANSFORMATIONS(validated_record)
      ADD_TO_LOAD_QUEUE(transformed_record)
    ELSE
      LOG_ERROR(record, "Validation Failed")
    END IF
  END FOR

  // LOAD PHASE
  load_queue = GET_LOAD_QUEUE()
  INSERT_INTO_WAREHOUSE(load_queue)
  UPDATE_METADATA("Last Load", CURRENT_TIMESTAMP)
  
  RETURN "ETL Completed Successfully"
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the three-phase ETL process: 1) Extracting data from source systems into staging, 2) Transforming and validating data, 3) Loading validated data into the warehouse and updating metadata.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of source records

examples.title: Simple Metadata Extraction Example
examples.description: Demonstrates how metadata can be extracted and stored from a data warehouse table
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates metadata management in a data warehouse, showing how to extract table/column metadata and track data lineage between source and target tables.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for metadata extraction per column, O(n) for n columns
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing metadata of n columns

examples.title: Data Warehouse Table Creation with Partitioning
examples.description: Demonstrates creating a fact table with partitioning strategy for better query performance
examples.code:
-- Create Fact Table with Range Partitioning
CREATE TABLE sales_fact (
    sale_id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,
    product_key INT NOT NULL,
    customer_key INT NOT NULL,
    date_key INT NOT NULL,
    store_key INT NOT NULL,
    quantity_sold INT,
    unit_price DECIMAL(10,2),
    total_amount DECIMAL(15,2),
    -- Surrogate keys for SCD Type 2
    effective_date DATE,
    expiration_date DATE,
    is_current CHAR(1) DEFAULT 'Y'
)
PARTITION BY RANGE (date_key) (
    PARTITION p2023 VALUES LESS THAN (20240101),
    PARTITION p2024 VALUES LESS THAN (20250101),
    PARTITION p2025 VALUES LESS THAN (20260101),
    PARTITION p_future VALUES LESS THAN MAXVALUE
);

-- Create Index for Partition Pruning
CREATE INDEX idx_sales_date ON sales_fact(date_key) LOCAL;
CREATE INDEX idx_sales_product ON sales_fact(product_key) GLOBAL;

-- Sample query that benefits from partitioning
SELECT 
    p.product_name,
    SUM(s.total_amount) AS total_revenue
FROM sales_fact s
JOIN dim_product p ON s.product_key = p.product_key
WHERE s.date_key BETWEEN 20240101 AND 20241231
GROUP BY p.product_name
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC;
examples.explanation: This SQL demonstrates creating a fact table with range partitioning by date. Partitioning improves query performance through partition pruning (only relevant partitions are scanned) and enables efficient data archival and management.
examples.timeComplexity: Query: O(n/p) where n is total rows, p is number of partitions scanned
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for table storage across partitions

examples.title: Data Warehouse Connection Pool Manager
examples.description: Demonstrates a connection pool implementation for efficient data warehouse access
examples.explanation: This Java code demonstrates a connection pool manager optimized for data warehouse access. Key features include: managing a pool of reusable connections, setting appropriate timeouts for long-running analytical queries, and proper transaction isolation levels for warehouse operations.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for connection retrieval from pool
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is MAX_POOL_SIZE for connection storage
